module.title: The FL Mixer
module.description: Master FL Studio's Mixer from the ground up — routing, insert chains, bus grouping, sidechain routing, and building templates that make every project start fast.

lessons.title: Mixer Architecture and Routing
lessons.body:
Every sound in FL Studio plays through the Master mixer track by default — which means every sound is mixed together with no individual control. The moment you want to EQ just the snare, compress just the bass, or control the volume of one element without affecting others, you need to route each instrument to its own mixer track. This lesson explains how the Mixer is structured and how routing works.

## The Mixer at a Glance

Open the Mixer with **F9**. You will see a row of vertical channel strips, each with:
- A **peak meter** showing the signal level
- A **fader** controlling output volume
- A **pan knob** controlling left-right position
- A **mute** button (green LED) and a **solo** button
- **Insert slots** (the chain of effect slots on the right panel) for plugins
- **Send knobs** at the bottom for routing to other mixer tracks

The leftmost track is always **Master** — every other track feeds into it by default. Tracks are numbered 1 upward. You can have up to 125 mixer tracks.

## Routing an Instrument to a Mixer Track

By default, every Channel Rack instrument sends its audio to the Master track directly. To route it to a specific mixer track:

1. Click the instrument channel in the Channel Rack to select it
2. Look at the **channel settings** area — there is a small mixer track number display (shows the track number it is currently routed to, usually 0 = Master)
3. Click that number and type the mixer track number you want, or left-click and scroll
4. Alternatively: in the Mixer, click the target mixer track to select it, then back in the Channel Rack, hold **Ctrl** and click the instrument — it routes to the selected mixer track

Once routed, the instrument's audio appears on that mixer track's meter instead of the Master.

## Naming and Colour-Coding

Double-click a mixer track name to rename it. Right-click the track name for colour options. An organised mixer is a working mixer — label every track before you start adding effects.

A consistent naming convention:
- **KDrum**, **Snare**, **HH-Closed**, **HH-Open**, **Perc** for drums
- **Bass**, **808** for low-end
- **Chords**, **Pad**, **Lead**, **Melody** for harmonic and melodic elements
- **FX**, **Riser**, **Atmos** for effects and atmosphere

## The Master Track

The Master track receives the summed output of all other mixer tracks and outputs to your audio interface. Insert effects on the Master apply to the entire mix — this is where your master bus processing goes (a limiter, a master EQ, mild compression).

Do not add heavy processing to the Master while mixing individual tracks — your mix decisions will be coloured by the master processing and you will compensate incorrectly. Add master processing only after the mix is balanced.

## Routing Multiple Instruments to One Mixer Track

You can route multiple Channel Rack instruments to the same mixer track. This is useful for:
- Layered sounds that always move together (two kick samples mixed as one)
- A group before bus processing (all percussion to one 'Perc' track)

To route multiple instruments to the same track, set each instrument's mixer track number to the same value.

## The Current Track Indicator

When you click a mixer track, it highlights green — this is the **current track**. Any Channel Rack instrument you click while holding **Ctrl** will route to the current mixer track. This is the fastest routing workflow: select the mixer track, then Ctrl+click instruments one by one to route them all.

## Checking Your Routing

Click any mixer track and look at the send/receive indicators at the bottom of the Mixer panel. Tracks that are sending to the selected track show a lit indicator. This lets you verify the full signal path at a glance.

Always route every instrument before adding any effects. An unrouted instrument playing through the Master cannot be EQ'd, compressed, or automated independently — it is mixed into everything permanently.
keyTakeaways: Every instrument must be routed to its own mixer track before you can EQ, compress, or automate it independently — the default Master routing gives you no per-instrument control.
The fastest routing workflow: click the target mixer track to select it (it highlights green), then Ctrl+click instruments in the Channel Rack to route them to it one by one.
Name and colour-code every mixer track before adding effects — an organised mixer is the difference between a session you can navigate and one you abandon.
lessons.tryThis: Open a project with at least four instruments all playing through the Master. Press F9 to open the Mixer. Select Mixer track 1, rename it 'Kick', and Ctrl+click your kick instrument in the Channel Rack — watch its signal appear on track 1's meter instead of the Master. Repeat for snare (track 2), bass (track 3), and chords (track 4). Now mute and solo individual tracks using the mixer buttons — notice how you now have complete per-instrument control that was impossible before routing.

lessons.title: Insert Effects and Signal Chain Order
lessons.body:
Once an instrument is routed to a mixer track, you can add effects to it using insert slots. The order those effects appear in the chain is not cosmetic — it changes the sound. Understanding signal chain order is the difference between a polished insert chain and one that undermines itself.

## What Insert Slots Are

On the right panel of any mixer track you will see a column of slots labelled 1 through 10 (expandable). Each slot can hold one plugin. The signal flows **top to bottom**: slot 1 processes first, its output feeds slot 2, and so on down the chain.

Click any empty slot and a plugin browser appears. Select a plugin to load it into that slot. The plugin receives the track's signal, processes it, and passes the result to the next slot.

## Why Order Matters

Consider two identical chains — EQ and compressor — in different orders:

**EQ → Compressor**: The compressor reacts to the EQ'd signal. If you boosted the low end with EQ, the compressor will react more aggressively to the low end. This is the most common arrangement for tracking and mixing — you shape the tone first, then control the dynamics of that shape.

**Compressor → EQ**: The compressor reacts to the unprocessed signal, then EQ shapes the result. Use this when you want the compressor to respond to the natural dynamics of the source — some engineers prefer this for drums, where the transient response of the compressor should be based on the unaltered hit.

Neither order is wrong. They sound different. Know which one you are using and why.

## A Standard Insert Chain by Instrument Type

**Drums (kick, snare)**
1. EQ (high-pass, remove mud, add click)
2. Compressor (control transients, add sustain)
3. Saturation/distortion (optional — add harmonic content)
4. EQ again (optional — small tonal tweaks post-compression)

**Bass / 808**
1. EQ (shape the tone, control boominess)
2. Compressor (control dynamics, tighten the sub)
3. Saturation (add upper harmonics so bass translates on small speakers)

**Synth leads and melodies**
1. EQ (high-pass, remove mud)
2. Compressor (even out note-to-note dynamics)
3. Reverb or delay (if not using a send/return)

**Pads and chords**
1. EQ (high-pass aggressively, remove low-mid mud)
2. Stereo widener (if the pad is mono or too narrow)
3. Reverb (light — heavy reverb usually goes on a send/return)

## Enabling and Disabling Insert Slots

Click the green LED on any insert slot to disable it — the plugin is bypassed but stays in the chain. This is the correct way to A/B an effect: disable it and listen, re-enable and listen. Do not remove and re-add the plugin.

## Slot Signal Flow Controls

Right-click an insert slot for advanced routing options:
- **Move up / Move down**: Reorder the slot in the chain without dragging
- **Replace**: Swap the plugin with a different one, keeping the same slot position
- **Save preset**: Save the current plugin state as a named preset

## Wet/Dry Mix on Inserts

Most plugins have an internal wet/dry knob. On insert slots, the plugin processes 100% of the signal by default. Use the plugin's own wet/dry (sometimes labelled Mix or Dry/Wet) to blend the processed and unprocessed signal:

- Reverb on an insert: set to 20–30% wet — the dry signal remains dominant
- Saturation: often 30–60% wet for subtle harmonic enrichment
- Compressor: no wet/dry concept — compression either applies or does not

## The Slot Count and CPU

Every active plugin in every insert slot uses CPU. On complex projects, disable insert slots you are not actively using. A slot with the LED off uses zero CPU. This is especially relevant for reverb and delay plugins, which are CPU-intensive.

## Reading the Signal Path

To understand exactly what is happening to a signal, click a mixer track and read the insert slots top to bottom. Each slot is a processing stage. The signal that exits the last active slot is what gets sent to the Master (or to whatever track it routes to). Keeping this chain clean, ordered, and purposeful is the foundation of a professional-sounding mix.
keyTakeaways: Insert slots process top to bottom — EQ before compressor shapes the tone first and lets the compressor react to the EQ'd signal; compressor before EQ lets the compressor respond to the raw dynamics.
Disable insert slots with the green LED rather than removing plugins — bypass lets you A/B the effect instantly without losing the plugin's settings.
A standard starting chain for most instruments: EQ (high-pass + cut problem frequencies) → Compressor → optional saturation → optional second EQ for post-compression tonal shaping.
lessons.tryThis: On your snare mixer track, add three insert slots in this order: Parametric EQ 2 → Fruity Limiter (COMP mode) → Fruity Soft Clipper. Set the EQ with a high-pass at 100 Hz and a gentle boost at 5 kHz. Set the compressor to 4:1 ratio. Now right-click the Parametric EQ 2 slot and select 'Move down' to put it after the compressor — play your loop and compare. Notice how the compressor responds differently when it receives the unboosted snare versus the pre-EQ'd version. Move the EQ back to the top and leave it there — that is the correct order for this use case.

lessons.title: Bus Routing and Track Groups
lessons.body:
Routing every track directly to the Master works for small projects. As soon as you have 15 or 20 tracks, you lose the ability to make group decisions — raising all the drums at once, adding glue compression to everything except the bass, muting the whole harmonic layer to hear how the rhythm sits. Bus routing solves all of this.

## What a Bus Track Is

A **bus track** (sometimes called a group track or aux track) is a mixer track that receives the output of multiple other tracks rather than receiving a direct instrument signal. You route several tracks into one bus, then process or control the whole group at the bus level.

The bus becomes a single point of control for everything routed into it:
- One fader raise lifts the entire drum group
- One compressor on the bus glues all the drums together
- One EQ cut on the bus removes mud from every drum simultaneously

## Setting Up a Bus in FL Studio

**Step 1: Create the bus track**
In the Mixer, pick an unused track and label it — "Drum Bus", "Melody Bus", "FX Bus".

**Step 2: Route individual tracks to the bus**
Click one of the source tracks (e.g. the Kick track). At the bottom of the Mixer, find the row of send knobs. Locate the send knob that corresponds to your bus track and turn it up to 100%. Also turn the Master send knob **down to 0** on the source track — otherwise the kick sends to both the bus and the Master, doubling the signal.

Repeat for every track you want in the group.

**Step 3: Route the bus to the Master**
The bus track should send to the Master at 100% (the default). Verify this — the Master send knob on the bus track should be at full.

## The Send Knob Default Behaviour

In FL Studio, every mixer track sends to the Master at 100% by default. When you add a bus:
- Turn the Master send **off** on source tracks routing to the bus (otherwise signal doubles)
- Keep the Master send **on** for the bus track itself

This is the most common mistake when setting up buses — forgetting to disable the direct Master send on the source tracks.

## Typical Bus Groups

| Bus Name | What Routes Into It |
|---|---|
| Drum Bus | Kick, snare, clap, hi-hats, percussion |
| Melody Bus | Lead, counter-melody, hooks |
| Harmonic Bus | Chords, pads, stabs |
| Bass Bus | 808, sub bass, mid bass |
| FX Bus | Risers, downlifters, atmospheres |
| Vocal Bus | All vocal layers (if applicable) |

## What to Put on a Bus

**Glue compression**: A bus compressor with a low ratio (2:1), moderate threshold, and slow attack/release unifies the transients of everything in the group — the drums start to sound like they were recorded together rather than assembled from separate samples. Fruity Limiter in COMP mode at 2:1 ratio with 3–5 dB reduction works well.

**Bus EQ**: Broad, gentle cuts on the bus address tonal problems across the group. A −2 dB cut at 300 Hz on the drum bus reduces the collective boxiness of kick, snare, and toms without touching each individually.

**Bus saturation**: Light saturation on a drum bus adds harmonic content and perceived punch. Fruity Soft Clipper at very low drive is a clean option.

**Bus fader for energy automation**: Automate the bus fader to raise the drum group slightly into the chorus — a 1–2 dB lift across the whole drum group lands with more impact than raising individual tracks.

## Nested Buses

You can route buses into other buses. A Kick track and Snare track route into a Drum Bus. The Drum Bus and Melody Bus and Harmonic Bus all route into a Full Mix Bus (not the Master). The Full Mix Bus routes to the Master. This nesting lets you process sub-groups at every level.

Do not over-nest. Two levels (individual track → group bus → Master) is sufficient for most productions. Three levels is justified only on very large sessions.

## Checking the Routing Graph

Select any mixer track. The send knobs at the bottom light up for every track the selected track is sending to. The mixer track numbers that light up when another track is selected show what is routing into that track. Use this to quickly verify the structure of a complex routing setup.
keyTakeaways: When routing a source track to a bus, turn the source track's Master send to zero — otherwise the signal reaches the Master twice (once direct, once through the bus) and the level doubles.
A glue compressor on the drum bus at 2:1 ratio with 3–5 dB reduction makes separately sampled drums sound like they were recorded in the same room — one of the highest-impact bus moves in any genre.
Two routing levels — individual track to group bus, group bus to Master — is enough for most projects; more nesting adds complexity without meaningful control benefit.
lessons.tryThis: Create a mixer track labelled 'Drum Bus'. Route your kick, snare, and hi-hat tracks into it: on each drum track, turn the send knob for 'Drum Bus' to 100% and turn the Master send to 0. Verify the Drum Bus itself still sends to Master at 100%. Add Fruity Limiter in COMP mode to the Drum Bus with ratio 2:1, threshold around −18 dB, and slow attack and release — aim for 3 dB of gain reduction. Now raise and lower the Drum Bus fader and hear the entire drum group move as one. Toggle the compressor on and off — hear how the glue compression tightens the kit.

lessons.title: Sidechain Routing
lessons.body:
Sidechaining is the technique of using one audio signal to control the processing of another. The most recognisable use is the pumping compression in house and electronic music — the bass or pads duck in volume every time the kick hits. But sidechain routing has subtler, equally important uses: controlling bass level relative to the kick, ducking reverb tails when the dry signal arrives, and de-essing vocals.

## How Sidechaining Works

A normal compressor watches its own input and compresses when that input exceeds the threshold. A sidechain compressor watches a **different** signal — the sidechain input — and uses that signal to trigger compression on its own track.

Result: the kick drum hits → the sidechain compressor on the bass track detects the kick → it compresses the bass momentarily → the bass ducks out of the way of the kick → both elements are clearly audible and the low end stays uncluttered.

## Setting Up a Sidechain in FL Studio

FL Studio uses a send-based sidechain system. The source track (the trigger — usually the kick) sends a copy of its signal to a sidechain input on the target track (the one being compressed).

**Step 1: Set up the source send**
Click the **kick drum** mixer track. At the bottom send row, find the mixer track number of the bass track. You do not want to send the kick's audio to the bass track's output — you want to send it as a sidechain signal only. Hold **Alt** while clicking the send knob for the bass track. This creates a sidechain send (shown in red) rather than a normal audio send.

**Step 2: Add a compressor to the target**
On the **bass** mixer track, add Fruity Peak Controller or Fruity Limiter to an insert slot.

**Using Fruity Peak Controller** (the FL-native approach):
1. Add Fruity Peak Controller to an insert slot on the bass track
2. In the Peak Controller, set the sidechain input to the kick's mixer track number
3. Right-click the bass track's volume fader and select **Link to controller**
4. In the link dialog, select Fruity Peak Controller as the source
5. Adjust the volume attenuation range — set the minimum value to determine how far the bass ducks

**Using Fruity Limiter with internal sidechain**:
1. Add Fruity Limiter to the bass insert chain
2. Click the **SC** (sidechain) tab in the limiter
3. Set the sidechain source to the mixer track number of the kick
4. On the COMP tab, set ratio to 4:1 or higher, fast attack (5 ms), moderate release (100–200 ms)
5. Adjust the threshold so the compressor activates only when the kick hits

## Sidechain Compression: Settings for the Pumping Effect

For the classic electronic pumping sound:
- **Ratio**: 8:1 or higher — heavy compression on each kick hit
- **Attack**: 1–5 ms — reacts almost instantly to the kick
- **Release**: Set to match the tempo. At 120 BPM (500 ms per beat), a release of 400–450 ms lets the bass return to full volume just before the next kick hit, creating the rhythmic pump
- **Threshold**: Set low enough that every kick hit triggers compression

For subtle low-end separation (not pumping, just clarity):
- **Ratio**: 3:1–4:1
- **Attack**: 10–20 ms (lets a tiny bit of the bass transient through)
- **Release**: 200–300 ms — faster recovery, less noticeable movement
- **Reduction**: Target 3–6 dB — audible but not dramatic

## Other Sidechain Applications

**Reverb ducking**: The reverb return track is sidechain-compressed by the dry signal. When the dry signal plays, the reverb compresses — the reverb tail does not wash over the attack of the next note. Release of 80–150 ms lets the reverb bloom between notes naturally.

**Pad ducking**: In dense harmonic arrangements, pads can fight with the kick and bass in the low-mids. Sidechain the pad track to the kick — 2:1 ratio, moderate reduction — and the pad steps back slightly on every beat, giving the low end room to punch through.

**De-essing**: A sidechain compressor triggered by the high-frequency content of a vocal (using an EQ to isolate the sibilant range in the sidechain path) compresses the full signal only when harsh S sounds occur — this is the principle behind every de-esser plugin.

## Sidechain Gain Staging

The sidechain trigger signal should be clean and at a consistent level. If the kick is too quiet, it will not trigger the compressor reliably. If it is clipping, the trigger is inaccurate. Use a well-gain-staged kick track (peaks around −12 dBFS) as your sidechain source.
keyTakeaways: Hold Alt while clicking a send knob to create a sidechain send (shown in red) rather than a normal audio send — the trigger signal routes to the compressor's sidechain input without mixing into the target track's audio output.

Set release time to match the tempo: at 120 BPM (500 ms per beat), a release of 400–450 ms returns the bass to full volume just before the next kick, creating the rhythmic pump that defines house and electronic music.
Sidechain compression is not only for pumping — subtle sidechain ducking of bass under kick (3–6 dB, fast attack, medium release) keeps low-end clarity without any audible pumping effect.
lessons.tryThis: On your kick mixer track, hold Alt and click the send knob for your bass track — the knob should turn red, indicating a sidechain send. Add Fruity Limiter to the bass insert chain, open the SC tab, and set the sidechain source to the kick track number. On the COMP tab, set ratio to 8:1, attack to 5 ms, and release to 400 ms. Lower the threshold until the gain reduction meter moves 6–10 dB on every kick hit. Play your loop and hear the bass duck rhythmically with each kick. Then try reducing the ratio to 3:1 and the release to 200 ms — compare the subtle clarity version against the pumping version.

lessons.title: Mixer Templates and Workflow
lessons.body:
Every time you start a new project and spend the first 30 minutes routing instruments, naming tracks, and setting up bus groups, you are doing the same work you did on the last project and the one before that. A mixer template eliminates that setup entirely so you can start making music immediately. This lesson shows you how to build one and how to develop a consistent mixer workflow.

## What a Mixer Template Is

A template is an FL Studio project file (.flp) saved with your standard mixer setup already in place — tracks named, colour-coded, buses routed, and starting insert plugins loaded. When you begin a new project, you open the template instead of a blank file.

## Building Your Template

Start from a blank project and set up the mixer exactly as you want it for every session:

**Standard track list (a starting point)**:

| Track | Name | Colour | Insert Plugins |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kick | Dark grey | Parametric EQ 2, Fruity Limiter |
| 2 | Snare | Light grey | Parametric EQ 2, Fruity Limiter |
| 3 | Clap | Light grey | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 4 | HH-Closed | Yellow | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 5 | HH-Open | Yellow | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 6 | Perc | Orange | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 7 | Drum Bus | Dark red | Fruity Limiter (glue comp) |
| 8 | 808 / Bass | Blue | Parametric EQ 2, Fruity Limiter |
| 9 | Sub | Blue | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 10 | Bass Bus | Dark blue | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 11 | Lead | Green | Parametric EQ 2, Fruity Limiter |
| 12 | Melody | Light green | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 13 | Chords | Purple | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 14 | Pad | Light purple | Parametric EQ 2 |
| 15 | Harmonic Bus | Dark purple | — |
| 16 | Atmos / FX | Teal | — |
| 17 | Reverb Return | White | Fruity Reeverb 2 (100% wet) |
| 18 | Delay Return | White | Fruity Delay 3 (100% wet) |

Set up the bus routing:
- Tracks 1–6 → Drum Bus (7), Master send off
- Tracks 8–9 → Bass Bus (10), Master send off
- Tracks 11–14 → Harmonic Bus (15), Master send off
- All buses → Master

Add a high-pass filter preset to every EQ instance — set to a sensible default for each track type (80 Hz for drums, 250 Hz for pads, 40 Hz for bass).

## Saving the Template

**File → Save as** — save to a location you will remember. Name it clearly: "My Template – Trap", "My Template – House", or just "Starter Template".

To use it: open the file, immediately **File → Save as** under a new name for your actual project. Never work directly in the template file or you will overwrite your starting point.

## Per-Genre Templates

Different genres use different instrument sets. Once you have one template working, save genre-specific variants:

- **Trap template**: 808 prominent, hi-hat patterns ready, pitched melody tracks
- **House template**: Four-on-the-floor kick routing, chord stab tracks, longer decay on the drum bus compressor
- **Lo-fi template**: Vinyl texture track pre-routed, warmer EQ defaults, less stereo width on the harmonic bus

## A Consistent Session Workflow

Beyond the template, develop a consistent order of operations for every session:

**1. Open template, save as new project name**
**2. Load sounds into Channel Rack instruments**
**3. Route each instrument to its pre-named mixer track** (using Ctrl+click)
**4. Gain-stage all tracks** before touching EQ or compression
**5. Build loops and patterns** in the Piano Roll
**6. Arrange in the Playlist**
**7. Mix** — EQ, compression, effects
**8. Automate** — transitions, energy moves
**9. Export** — full WAV, reference on multiple systems

This order matters. Mixing before arranging means you are EQ'ing elements in a context that will change. Automating before the mix is balanced means your automation curves will need revision. Following the same order every session builds muscle memory and keeps decisions in the right context.

## Keeping the Template Current

After every five or so projects, review the template:
- Are there tracks you never use? Remove them.
- Are there tracks you always add manually? Add them to the template.
- Have your default EQ settings improved? Update the template presets.

A template is a living document, not a fixed one. The goal is to reduce the distance between opening FL Studio and making music.
keyTakeaways: A mixer template is just a saved .flp with your standard routing and insert plugins already in place — open it, immediately Save As a new project name, and you are ready to make music without any setup.

Follow the same order of operations every session: route → gain stage → build loops → arrange → mix → automate → export. Mixing before arranging or automating before mixing leads to decisions made in the wrong context.
Review and update your template every few projects — remove tracks you never use, add ones you always create manually, and update default plugin settings as your workflow improves.
lessons.tryThis: Build a minimal starter template right now. Create a blank FL Studio project and set up eight mixer tracks: Kick, Snare, HH, Drum Bus, Bass, Lead, Chords, and Pad. Route the first three to the Drum Bus (Alt+send to turn off Master send on each). Add Parametric EQ 2 to every track and set sensible high-pass defaults. Colour-code by group. Save it as 'Starter Template.flp' in a dedicated folder. Open a current project you are working on, compare its messy mixer to your template, and identify which tracks are missing — add those to the template before the next session.
